text: The surgeon addresses the recurrent umbilical hernia by dissecting the preperitoneal fat and hernia sac, encountering thick, scarred tissue indicative of a recurrent condition. The hernia sac is successfully taken down, but a full-thickness burn of the umbilical skin occurs due to misidentification of tissue layers, leading to excision and reconstruction of the umbilicus. The surgeon proceeds to dissect the contralateral side, carefully navigating the preperitoneal fat and peritoneum using a cut-push technique, minimizing tension and avoiding perforations. Cold dissection is employed to complete the process before selecting and placing the mesh in the pre-peritoneal pocket.

The surgeon closes the defect in two layers using a running non-absorbable number one PDS suture. The surgeon struggles with the eighteen-inch suture length but continues to work on the inferior aspect of the pocket to ensure the fifteen by fifteen centimeter flat polypropylene uncoated mesh lays flat. Additional dissection is performed, followed by placement of eight tacking sutures, including two Vicryl sutures and three on each side, with two more centrally to secure the mesh against the fascia. A three-oh barbed suture is used to close the pocket.
